nodes.label: Claude Fable 5 Lite
nodes.rationale: Community name for Opus 4.8 weights running Fable 5's leaked product harness: load the Fable 5 system prompt into Opus 4.8 and set effort to high. Behavioral (not capability) upgrade.

nodes.label: Fable-5 to Opus-4.8 fallback pattern
nodes.rationale: When the preferred Fable 5 model becomes unavailable (suspended), fall back to Anthropic's next-most-capable available model Opus 4.8 while preserving the Fable 5 instruction harness — the closest substitute since the prompt surface and instruction-processing architecture are shared.

nodes.label: Claude Fable 5
nodes.rationale: Mythos-class model released June 9 2026 with 1M-token context, adaptive thinking on by default; best-in-class on software engineering, scientific reasoning, vision, long-horizon agentic work. Live ~72 hours before suspension.

nodes.label: Claude Opus 4.8
nodes.rationale: Anthropic's next-most-capable model still available after Fable 5 suspension; the fallback target that runs the same prompt surface and instruction-processing architecture.

nodes.label: Fable 5 system prompt (leaked)
nodes.rationale: 1,585-line operating instruction set covering identity, tool schemas, refusal logic, design instincts, citation rules, memory behavior; leaked within hours of suspension. The one reusable piece of the stack.

nodes.label: Fable 5 export-control suspension
nodes.rationale: June 12 2026 U.S. export-control directive forced Anthropic to suspend access for all users; this unavailability is what motivates the fallback pattern.

nodes.label: Instruction-layer transfer
nodes.rationale: What actually transfers between models via the prompt is the instruction layer — identity, autonomy defaults, frontend-design instincts, tool-use posture, response style — not raw model intelligence. Behavioral delta is large on design/agentic/structured tasks, zero on raw-intelligence benchmarks.

nodes.label: Capability vs instruction distinction
nodes.rationale: Not a true capability unlock: only original Fable 5 weights deliver Mythos-class gains. Loading the prompt into Opus 4.8 transfers product harness/behavior but not the underlying model capability.

nodes.label: Effort set to high
nodes.rationale: Setting reasoning effort to high is a required step of the Fable 5 Lite recipe — pairs the Fable 5 harness with maximal Opus 4.8 reasoning.

nodes.label: Shared prompt surface portability
nodes.rationale: Opus 4.8 runs against the same prompt surface as Fable 5, so the leaked prompt drops in unchanged: the API system field, project instructions on claude.ai, and --append-system-prompt-file on Claude Code.

nodes.label: Prompt-injection surfaces (system field / project instructions / append-system-prompt-file)
Shared instruction-processing architecture
nodes.rationale: Opus 4.8 follows the same instruction-processing architecture as Fable 5, which is why the same system prompt yields comparable behavior.

nodes.label: A/B verification matrix
nodes.rationale: Head-to-head comparison of vanilla Opus 4.8 vs Opus 4.8 + Fable 5 prompt on the same brief; outputs looked like products from different companies — the verification/oracle step confirming the harness effect.

nodes.label: Advisor-Executor Pattern
nodes.rationale: A multi-agent optimization technique that splits reasoning and execution across two different models rather than routing everything through one, so the premium model is used only for high-level planning and a cheaper model does the bulk execution.

nodes.label: Fable 5 as Advisor
nodes.rationale: Fable 5 (Anthropic's most capable model tier) does the thinking: analyzes the problem, proposes a structured plan, flags edge cases, and produces a precise specification. Used only for high-level reasoning to conserve its scarce allocation.

nodes.label: Opus/Sonnet as Executor
nodes.rationale: The executor receives the advisor's specification and executes it (writing code, modifying files, running commands) without re-deriving strategy; Opus/Sonnet are faster, cheaper, and reliably capable of following a clear plan.

nodes.label: Fable 5 Token Budget Problem
nodes.rationale: Fable 5's usage limits mean allocation is burned fast when it both plans and executes every task; default Claude Code behavior uses the premium model for everything (every file read, code write, minor formatting), which is wasteful and exhausts the daily limit before large tasks finish.

nodes.label: Cost Routing Across Model Classes
nodes.rationale: Route the reasoning phase (5-15% of tokens) to the frontier model and the execution phase (85-95% of tokens) to a cheaper model, so you pay frontier prices only for the small reasoning share and the bulk runs at lower per-token cost, stretching the Fable 5 allocation across far more tasks.

nodes.label: Reasoning vs Execution Token Split
nodes.rationale: In typical coding tasks the reasoning phase (understand codebase, decide strategy, identify edge cases) is ~5-15% of total token usage, and the execution phase (writing/modifying/reviewing code) is ~85-95%. This distribution is the numerical basis for the '93% of work at a fraction of cost' claim.

nodes.label: 93% Efficiency Framing
nodes.rationale: Roughly 93% of the work gets done at a fraction of the token cost because only the 5-15% reasoning phase runs at frontier (Fable 5) pricing while the 85-95% execution runs on the cheaper executor model.

nodes.label: Architect-Builder Analogy
nodes.rationale: Separating the architect from the builders: the architect (Fable 5) draws up detailed plans once; the builders (Opus/Sonnet) implement those plans across dozens of tasks without the architect being on-site for every nail.

nodes.label: Advise-Execute-Spotcheck-Readvise Loop
nodes.rationale: The operating loop: advise -> execute -> spot-check -> re-advise if needed. Keeps Fable 5 usage concentrated on reasoning while execution costs land on the cheaper model; re-advising is a compact planning call, not a full re-run.

nodes.label: Structured Plan Specification
nodes.rationale: The advisor emits a compact structured output (JSON, YAML, or numbered lists) the executor can parse reliably; being specific about plan contents keeps the advisor's response compact and actionable.

nodes.label: Claude Code --model Flag
nodes.rationale: Claude Code supports multi-model workflows via its --model flag (e.g. --model claude-fable-5 for advising, --model claude-sonnet-4 for executing) and structured prompt chaining, which is the mechanism that implements the pattern.

nodes.label: Save Advisor Output to File
nodes.rationale: Capture the advisor's plan to a file (e.g. migration-plan.json/yaml) in the working directory so the executor session can load it as context and progress can be tracked and resumed.

nodes.label: Do Not Deviate From Plan Constraint
nodes.rationale: Instructing the executor 'do not deviate from the plan' prevents Sonnet from re-deriving strategy, which would waste tokens and introduce inconsistency — the executor should follow instructions, not strategize.

nodes.label: Note Issues and Continue Constraint
nodes.rationale: The executor flags problems and continues rather than stopping for decisions it isn't equipped to handle; those flagged decisions are routed back to the Fable 5 advisor later. Keeps execution moving without escalating cost.

nodes.label: Progress Tracking Checkpoint
nodes.rationale: Having the executor mark each item complete in the plan file as it goes gives a resumable checkpoint if a session ends, supporting long-running multi-file tasks that outlast a single session.

nodes.label: Review and Re-Advise Step
nodes.rationale: When the executor finishes or flags issues, a short Fable 5 session reviews edge cases, handles exceptions, or updates the plan — a compact planning call rather than a full re-run, forming the verification/re-planning arm of the loop.

nodes.label: TypeScript Migration Example
nodes.rationale: A 40+ file Node.js-to-TypeScript migration where Fable 5 end-to-end would exhaust the daily limit after 10-15 files; the pattern uses one compact Fable 5 planning session plus Sonnet executing the remaining 90%+ file-by-file.

nodes.label: React Class-to-Hooks Refactor Example
nodes.rationale: Converting 28 React class components to functional components with hooks: Fable 5 used ~4,200 tokens (analysis only), Sonnet ~38,000 tokens (execution across 28 files), consuming <15% of a typical daily Fable 5 allocation; all 28 converted with 3 flagged for manual review, versus 40,000+ Fable 5 tokens for a single-model run.

nodes.label: Codex Orchestration
nodes.rationale: A Codex plugin that adds four roles (Planner, Advisor, Designer, Executor) to a task so different models handle planning, review, design, and implementation; the selected Codex model stays root, passes work between roles, checks results, and gives the final answer.

nodes.label: Codex as root orchestrator
nodes.rationale: The model selected for the Codex task remains in charge and is the final authority: it coordinates the work, passes work between roles, checks every result, and decides when delegation or parallel work is useful. Roles report only to Codex, never to each other.

nodes.label: Planner role
nodes.rationale: Optional role that creates the first plan and improves it after Advisor feedback; when omitted, the current Codex model plans. Typically Claude Fable 5.

nodes.label: Advisor role
nodes.rationale: Optional role that reviews the plan, finds important gaps, and approves it (returns PLAN_APPROVED) when ready. Advisor approval is a planning gate, not a guarantee that implementation will succeed.

nodes.label: Designer role
nodes.rationale: Optional role that turns approved requirements into a bounded visual/UX/interaction/IA/design-system handoff. May edit only design artifacts explicitly delegated by Codex; never changes implementation code or releases Executor.

nodes.label: Executor role
nodes.rationale: Required role that implements the approved plan. Multiple executors can run independent implementation work in parallel.

nodes.label: Planner-Advisor revision loop
nodes.rationale: Planner and Advisor iterate through several revisions: Advisor reviews, if it needs work Planner improves it and resubmits. Codex stops as soon as the Advisor returns PLAN_APPROVED. A verification/review-oracle loop for plans before code changes begin.

nodes.label: PLAN_APPROVED gate
nodes.rationale: Codex stops the review loop as soon as the Advisor returns PLAN_APPROVED. If approval is not reached within the limit, execution stops and Codex shows the latest plan and unresolved issues.

nodes.label: Five-review safety limit
nodes.rationale: The Planner-Advisor loop has a hard safety limit of five reviews to bound iteration; if approval is not reached, execution stops rather than looping indefinitely — a fail-closed control on the autonomous review loop.

nodes.label: Independent-review model constraint
nodes.rationale: Planner and Advisor must use different configured model routes so the plan review is independent — a distinct model reviewing avoids self-approval bias.

nodes.label: Claude Fable 5 as Planner/Advisor
nodes.rationale: Fable 5 is brought into Codex via the official Claude Code CLI and first-party Claude login (no Anthropic API key needed), used directly as Planner or Advisor. It is the sealed subscription/bundled cross-provider exception.

nodes.label: Reasoning-effort routing (Low..Max/Ultra)
nodes.rationale: Each role's model is configured with a reasoning effort: Low, Medium, High, XHigh, or Max. Fable defaults to High. Ultra is accepted as an alias for Max because Claude Code does not expose a separate Ultra effort.

nodes.label: Premium-model limit offload (~40% fewer)
nodes.rationale: Moving repeatable work away from the root model can hit premium-model limits ~40% less often — a rate-limit/quota-exhaustion mitigation. Figures are targets not guarantees; results depend on models, task, context, retries, and parallel work.

nodes.label: Parallel executor implementation (up to 2x)
nodes.rationale: Independent implementation work runs in parallel across executors, up to 2x faster on suitable tasks. Codex decides when parallel work is useful; speed figures are targets not guarantees.

nodes.label: External Model roles
nodes.rationale: External Models are roles, not picker entries. Codex stays root signed in with ChatGPT; the plugin adds only provider-pinned personal agents per validated effort, never changing top-level model or model_provider. E.g. OpenRouter moonshotai/kimi-k3 as researcher/designer.

nodes.label: External Model registry
nodes.rationale: The plugin checks its External Model registry for availability instead of guessing from the visible tool list, distinguishing whether a model is bundled/supported, configured on this installation, and callable in the current task. Availability questions are read-only status inspection.

nodes.label: Gate 0 billable qualification probe
nodes.rationale: Each installation stays unqualified/uncallable until its exact provider/model/effort tuple passes an explicitly billable isolated Gate 0 probe, separately authorized. Staged setup: preview adapter, authenticate via hidden OS-credential-store prompt, approve Gate 0, create role variants, start a new task. Never paste keys into chat.

nodes.label: Sealed tool-free codex exec transport
nodes.rationale: For READY external roles (v0.8.0+) the plugin invokes the model through a sealed, tool-free `codex exec` transport with the task packet only on stdin; it never executes an External Model through native agents.spawn_agent. Isolation/security boundary for delegation.

nodes.label: Fable bridge caller boundary
nodes.rationale: The workflow reserves Fable planning tools for the root Codex model by policy. Because current MCP calls do not identify their caller, the caller boundary is instruction-enforced; the bridge itself still disables tools, edits, and session persistence.

nodes.label: Role isolation / no cross-talk policy
nodes.rationale: Planner, Advisor, and Designer report only to Codex and never contact one another or Executors directly. Role labels are literal and Codex must never move a model to a different role. Keeps the orchestrator as the single coordination hub.

nodes.label: Bounded design handoff
nodes.rationale: Designer produces an optional bounded visual/UX/interaction/IA/design-system handoff from approved requirements, inserted between plan approval and executor implementation.

nodes.label: Codex tests & delivers stage
nodes.rationale: Final workflow stage: after executors implement, Codex tests and delivers the result — the verification-and-delivery step owned by the root orchestrator.

nodes.label: 'no subagents' user directive
nodes.rationale: If the user says `no subagents`, Codex must not delegate. Gives the user an explicit override on the delegation/parallelism the orchestrator would otherwise choose.

nodes.label: Production-readiness workflow
nodes.rationale: Repository discipline: preflight quick/full checks are source of truth (local PARTIAL, hosted checks authoritative); an exact regression test per behavior fix; payload changes require a strictly greater semver; security/state changes require a threat model, malformed/negative-path tests, and a fresh final-tree review bound to the exact head SHA; fail closed when state or authority is ambiguous.

nodes.label: Architect pattern (advisor-executor)
nodes.rationale: The smartest model runs the show and cheaper models do the typing: the session runs as a full-time architect owning requirements, decomposition, specs, and verification, routing every implementation task to the cheapest adequate lane.

nodes.label: Fable 5 architect session
nodes.rationale: The session itself runs on Fable 5 (Anthropic's most capable model) acting as a full-time architect; started via /model fable. It emits judgment and specs, never types code.

nodes.label: Cheapest-adequate-lane routing table
nodes.rationale: Three lanes: Routine (Grok 4.5, default) when the spec fully determines the outcome; Cross-vendor (GPT-5.6 Sol high reasoning) for correctness-critical or second implementation; Judgment (Fable 5) at commitment boundaries.

nodes.label: Token-volume routing rationale
nodes.rationale: Tokens route by volume: the expensive model emits the fewest tokens (judgment and specs), cheap lanes emit the most (code). Implementation mechanics are ~90% of a session's tokens and Grok 4.5 handles them at near-parity, so this runs far cheaper than Fable-for-everything.

nodes.label: Cost discipline: emit judgment not volume
nodes.rationale: Keeps the expensive model's own token volume minimal: emit judgment not volume, keep context lean, reason once then hand off. Spend the premium where judgment lives.

nodes.label: Five-part spec contract
nodes.rationale: The five-part spec contract makes context-free delegation safe — the architect writes a complete spec so a subagent can implement without the architect's conversation context.

nodes.label: Context-free delegation
nodes.rationale: Implementer subagents receive a self-contained spec rather than the architect's accumulated conversation, keeping the architect's context lean and delegation safe.

nodes.label: Verification rules keep cheap lanes honest
nodes.rationale: The architect reads the diff and verification evidence when the report comes back and only then reports done; verification rules keep cheap lanes honest. Verify evidence before accepting any lane's report.

nodes.label: Orchestration skill
nodes.rationale: The routing doctrine that teaches the session when to use each lane, the cost discipline, the five-part spec contract, and the verification rules. Shipped by the plugin.

nodes.label: grok-implementer agent (Grok 4.5)
nodes.rationale: Default typing lane; drives Grok 4.5 headlessly (grok --prompt-file … -m grok-4.5). Reports STATUS: unavailable rather than silently falling back to a Claude model.

nodes.label: codex-implementer agent (GPT-5.6 Sol)
nodes.rationale: Optional cross-vendor lane; invokes GPT-5.6 Sol as gpt-5.6-sol with model_reasoning_effort=high for correctness-critical work or a second independent implementation.

nodes.label: fable-advisor agent
nodes.rationale: Read-only second-opinion advisor running Fable 5; consulted at commitment boundaries; reads actual code, returns a verdict under 300 words, never implements. Tools: Read, Grep, Glob.

nodes.label: Grok CLI (xAI)
nodes.rationale: Headless CLI the grok-implementer needs, installed and authenticated (grok login); drives Grok 4.5.

nodes.label: OpenAI Codex CLI
nodes.rationale: CLI the codex-implementer needs (npm i -g @openai/codex, codex login); invokes GPT-5.6 Sol.

nodes.label: Racing implementer lanes on one spec
nodes.rationale: For high-stakes work, race grok-implementer and codex-implementer on the same spec and let the architect pick the stronger diff.

nodes.label: Cross-vendor review built into routing
nodes.rationale: Every implementation comes from a different model family than the architect that reviews it, so cross-vendor review is built into the routing, not bolted on — the architect stays Claude, the lanes are producers not judges.

nodes.label: Vendor diversity avoids shared blind spots
nodes.rationale: Models from one family share blind spots; an independent implementation from a different lineage catches what same-family review misses.

nodes.label: Commitment boundaries
nodes.rationale: The moments that decide whether the next hour of work is wasted: architecture decisions, data migrations, API designs, refactor strategy, and whenever a problem has resisted two attempts.

nodes.label: Read-only skeptic role
nodes.rationale: Advises only, never implements/edits/writes files; looks at the actual code before opining; never rubber-stamps and pushes back genuinely when warranted. Running it from a Fable session still pays: it sees code fresh without the conversation's accumulated assumptions.

nodes.label: Advisor-only (Lite) mode
nodes.rationale: The inverse arrangement: keep the session cheap (Sonnet) and consult fable-advisor only at commitment boundaries; one-file install into ~/.claude/agents/.

nodes.label: Claude Code model resolution order
nodes.rationale: CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL env var → per-invocation model parameter → agent frontmatter → session model. This is how each subagent can run on a different model than the session.

nodes.label: Quiet fallback for unavailable Claude pins
nodes.rationale: If a pinned Claude model isn't available, Claude Code silently falls back to the session model — the pattern degrades quietly rather than erroring; applies only to Claude model pins.

nodes.label: Opus fallback for non-Fable accounts
nodes.rationale: With no Fable access (e.g. API-key billing), use /model opus for the session and change model: fable → model: opus in the advisor file; same pattern, model tiers shift down one.

nodes.label: Non-Claude lanes fail loudly
nodes.rationale: The grok and codex lanes always fail loudly with a structured STATUS: unavailable error and never silently fall back to a Claude model, unlike Claude model pins.

nodes.label: Verdict-not-survey answer discipline
nodes.rationale: Give 'Do X, not Y, because Z' and name the single risk that decides it; a sound plan gets one line; missing information is named precisely; stay under ~300 words for a model reader mid-task.

nodes.label: ~300-word verdict budget
nodes.rationale: The reader is another model mid-task, not a human reading a report, so the verdict stays under ~300 words.

nodes.label: SkillOpt
nodes.rationale: Treats a natural-language skill document as the trainable state of a frozen agent, training it with deep-learning discipline (epochs, learning rate, validation gates) WITHOUT touching model weights — because hand-crafted or one-shot-generated skills don't reliably improve over their starting point under feedback.

nodes.label: Skill Document as Trainable State
nodes.rationale: The skill Markdown doc is the analogue of model weights; the frozen target model is unchanged. This adds ZERO inference-time model calls at deployment — the deployed artifact is a compact best_skill.md (300-2000 tokens) that runs against the unchanged target.

nodes.label: Self-Evolution Training Loop
nodes.rationale: The core loop: rollout -> reflect -> aggregate -> select -> update -> evaluate, with an epoch boundary carrying slow update and meta skill memory. This is the self-improvement loop that iteratively improves the skill under scored feedback.

nodes.label: Rollout Stage
nodes.rationale: The target model executes tasks; analogue of the forward pass in deep learning. Produces scored trajectories the optimizer analyzes.

nodes.label: Reflect Stage
nodes.rationale: A separate optimizer model analyzes trajectories and produces bounded add/delete/replace edit patches; analogue of loss/gradient computation.

nodes.label: Aggregate Stage
nodes.rationale: Merges edit patches from multiple reflected trajectories into a combined candidate edit set.

nodes.label: Select / Edit Selection Stage
nodes.rationale: Ranks and clips edits; analogue of gradient clipping. The learning_rate = max number of edits accepted, bounding how far the skill moves per step for training stability.

nodes.label: Update Stage
nodes.rationale: Applies the selected patch to the skill document; analogue of an SGD step.

nodes.label: Held-out Validation Gate
nodes.rationale: In the paper-style path a candidate edit is accepted ONLY when it strictly improves a held-out validation score on the selection split. This gate is what makes skill training reliably improve over its starting point rather than drift under loosely-controlled self-revision.

nodes.label: Textual Learning-Rate Budget
nodes.rationale: learning_rate caps the maximum number of edits per update (gradient clipping analogue), bounding how far the skill can move each step; combined with a rejected-edit buffer and epoch-wise slow/meta update it keeps skill training stable.

nodes.label: LR Scheduler
nodes.rationale: lr_scheduler supports cosine, linear, and constant schedules for the edit budget over training, mirroring deep-learning learning-rate schedules.

nodes.label: Rejected-Edit Buffer
nodes.rationale: Buffers edits rejected by the validation gate; a stabilizing mechanism that (with slow/meta update) keeps skill training from thrashing.

nodes.label: Epoch-wise Slow Update
nodes.rationale: An epoch-boundary slow update that stabilizes multi-epoch skill training, analogous to a momentum/EMA slow-weight update.

nodes.label: Meta Skill Memory
nodes.rationale: An epoch-boundary meta-skill memory mechanism carrying accumulated learning across epochs, complementing the slow update.

nodes.label: Epoch Boundary
nodes.rationale: Multi-epoch training boundary at which slow update and meta skill memory are applied.

nodes.label: Optimizer / Target Role Separation
nodes.rationale: Optimizer and target model roles are configured separately with per-role backend overrides. The optimizer turns scored rollouts into edits; the frozen target executes tasks. This delegation (a planner/optimizer model vs an executor/target model) is the model-routing knob of SkillOpt.

nodes.label: best_skill.md Deployed Artifact
nodes.rationale: The compact 300-2000 token Markdown skill produced by training; runs against the unchanged target model and transfers across model scales, between Codex and Claude Code harnesses, and to nearby benchmarks without further optimization.

nodes.label: Deep-Learning Training Analogy
nodes.rationale: Maps weights->skill doc, forward pass->rollout, loss/gradient->reflect, gradient clipping->edit selection, SGD step->patch apply, validation set->gated eval, LR schedule->lr_scheduler, epochs->slow update+meta skill. Borrowing this discipline is what makes skill optimization reproducible.

nodes.label: SkillOpt-Sleep
nodes.rationale: A nightly offline self-evolution engine for local coding agents (Claude Code / Codex / Copilot): harvest -> mine -> replay -> consolidate behind a held-out validation gate. Shipped as the skillopt-sleep CLI; stages proposed memory/skill updates for human adoption rather than auto-applying — a long-running autonomous self-improvement loop with a human-adoption safety boundary.

nodes.label: Harvest-Mine-Replay-Consolidate Cycle
nodes.rationale: SkillOpt-Sleep's offline cycle: review past sessions (harvest), mine recurring tasks, replay them, and consolidate validated skills behind a held-out gate. The nightly-batch analogue of the online training loop.

nodes.label: Two Complementary Workflows
nodes.rationale: Research engine (skillopt-train / skillopt-eval, benchmark splits) vs SkillOpt-Sleep (skillopt-sleep, real coding-agent sessions). They share bounded-text-update + validation but are separate entry points with different configs and safety boundaries.

nodes.label: Backend Abstraction
nodes.rationale: A backend is a chat/exec target (openai_chat, claude_chat, qwen_chat, minimax_chat, openai_compatible, codex_exec, claude_code_exec, cursor_exec). Providers implementing OpenAI Chat Completions should use the generic openai_compatible backend before adding code.

nodes.label: Claude Code / Codex Exec Harness
nodes.rationale: Codex and Claude Code exec harnesses are target-only (share codex_harness.py). SkillOpt evaluates skills inside agentic loops — direct chat, Codex CLI, Claude Code CLI. Note: claude_chat launches `claude -p`, not a direct Anthropic API client.

nodes.label: Zero Inference-Time Model Calls
nodes.rationale: All optimization cost is paid at training time; at deployment the skill adds zero extra model calls — the compact skill doc is simply prepended to the unchanged target, so runtime token/budget cost is minimal.

nodes.label: Skill Transfer / Generalization
nodes.rationale: Optimized skill artifacts transfer across model scales, between Codex and Claude Code harnesses, and to nearby benchmarks without further optimization — best or tied-best on all 52 (model, benchmark, harness) cells, +23.5/+24.8/+19.1 point lifts on GPT-5.5 in direct chat / Codex / Claude Code.

nodes.label: Fable Orchestrator plugin
nodes.rationale: Claude Code plugin exploiting per-subagent model routing: the smartest model runs the show, cheaper models do the typing.

nodes.label: Architect pattern
nodes.rationale: Session runs on the most capable model as a full-time architect owning requirements, decomposition, specs, routing, and verification while delegating typing to cross-vendor lanes.

nodes.label: Fable 5 architect session
nodes.rationale: Fable 5 (Anthropic's most capable model) is the session/orchestrator model; owns the hard parts (judgment, specs) and verifies results. Falls back to /model opus when no Fable access.

nodes.label: Token-volume routing
nodes.rationale: Tokens route by volume: the expensive architect emits the fewest (judgment and specs), CLI producers emit the most (code), a thin Sonnet wrapper supervises each lane.

nodes.label: Economic case for delegation
nodes.rationale: CLI lanes need only be good enough when the architect owns the hard parts and verifies; runs far cheaper than Fable-for-everything. Spend the premium where judgment lives.

nodes.label: Cross-vendor CLI lanes
nodes.rationale: Detached CLI producer lanes (Grok 4.5, GPT-5.6 Sol) that do the bulk typing; producers, not judges. The architect stays Claude.

nodes.label: grok-implementer (Grok 4.5)
nodes.rationale: Grok 4.5 implementation lane; default in grok mode. Runs bypassPermissions with enforced deny rules; edits files and runs verification headlessly.

nodes.label: codex-implementer (GPT-5.6 Sol)
nodes.rationale: GPT-5.6 Sol at model_reasoning_effort=high; correctness-critical implementation lane. Sandbox workspace-write, never danger-full-access.

nodes.label: Implementation lane mode (grok/codex/mix)
nodes.rationale: One CLAUDE.md line selects routing: grok (default) everything to Grok; codex everything to GPT-5.6 Sol high reasoning; mix routes per task. Modes change routing only — spec/verification/review rules identical.

nodes.label: mix mode routing
nodes.rationale: Architect routes each task by kind: mechanical/spec-determined → grok; correctness-critical (concurrency, auth, migrations, subtle state) or in doubt → codex.

nodes.label: Claude Opus terminal fallback
nodes.rationale: A guaranteed Claude Opus subagent is always the final implementation fallback when both CLI lanes are down; also the cold-review fallback when a reviewer lane is unavailable.

nodes.label: Lane fallback chain
nodes.rationale: Fallbacks mirror by mode: an unavailable implementation lane re-routes to the other CLI lane if installed, then the Opus subagent as terminal fallback; an unavailable reviewer falls to a cold Opus pass. Availability is discovered, not declared.

nodes.label: Announced substitution / fail loudly
nodes.rationale: Every substitution is announced; verification never relaxed; review never silently skipped. A missing CLI fails loudly with a structured error, never a silent substitution — except Claude model pins, which Claude Code silently falls back to the session model.

nodes.label: Verification is not review
nodes.rationale: Verification asks 'did it do what the spec said and do the checks pass?' (architect does it on every diff with cross-vendor eyes). Cold review asks 'what's wrong the author didn't see?' — a separate reviewer pass. Two distinct assurance layers.

nodes.label: Architect verification loop
nodes.rationale: Architect reads the diff, checks execution evidence (captured log or re-run), verifies against the spec on every diff. One authoritative verification run per task; spot-checks reports and re-runs at integration points.

nodes.label: Cold review of a behavior-bearing diff
nodes.rationale: Diff-only, no intent framing, every claim cited file:line. Security/auth/concurrency paths add a silent-failure completeness read on a strong Claude model.

nodes.label: Opposite-family review rule
nodes.rationale: Cold review always comes from the model family that didn't write the diff; a same-family reviewer shares the author's blind spots. Falls back to a cold Opus pass if that lane is down.

nodes.label: grok-reviewer
nodes.rationale: Grok 4.5 diff-only reviewer, used when codex or Claude implemented. Hard read-only: read_file/grep/list_dir allowlist, no shell, no edit tools.

nodes.label: codex-reviewer
nodes.rationale: GPT-5.6 Sol diff-only reviewer, used when grok implemented. codex exec review with sandbox_mode read-only; derives the diff from a ref itself, cannot write.

nodes.label: Opus cold review fallback
nodes.rationale: When a reviewer CLI lane is down, review falls back to a cold Opus pass (Claude is a third family versus both CLI implementers). Also the single-CLI degraded path.

nodes.label: Never race lanes
nodes.rationale: Racing pays twice for typing plus once more for judging, and a plausible-looking wrong diff still needs review to catch. One implementation plus independent cross-vendor review buys the same assurance cheaper.

nodes.label: One lane per task
nodes.rationale: Each task goes to exactly one implementation lane; assurance comes from verification plus tiered review of the diff, not from duplicate implementations.

nodes.label: fable-advisor agent
nodes.rationale: Read-only skeptic (model: fable, tools Read/Grep/Glob) giving a second opinion at commitment boundaries. Returns a verdict under 300 words, names the single deciding risk, never implements; declines to rule on evidence it wasn't given.

nodes.label: Commitment boundary consult
nodes.rationale: Advisor is consulted before architecture decisions, migrations, API designs, big refactors, and whenever a problem has resisted two failed attempts. Given the decision, constraints, options, files to read, and decisive evidence pasted in.

nodes.label: scripts/run-lane.sh supervisor
nodes.rationale: Launches each CLI lane detached with process-group supervision and a pure-bash watchdog (no coreutils). The wall clock holds even if the supervising agent dies; test-run-lane.sh smoke-tests it without API calls.

nodes.label: Long-run survival
nodes.rationale: Lanes launch detached so long runs survive; foreground supervision would otherwise be killed mid-run on exactly the tasks worth delegating.

nodes.label: 10-minute foreground tool cap
nodes.rationale: The Claude Code harness caps any foreground tool call at 10 minutes, which would kill supervision mid-run; detached process-group supervision is the workaround.

nodes.label: Lanes run in the background
nodes.rationale: Architect leaves the Agent tool's background default alone, ends its turn with a one-line status, and acts on the completion notification. A sequential dependency is not a reason to lock the user out; synchronous waits are a rare announced exception.

nodes.label: Completion notification handling
nodes.rationale: The architect acts on the lane's completion notification; the wrapper's structured report is the only signal a lane is settled.

nodes.label: Completion without a report is not success
nodes.rationale: Without the wrapper's structured report (with reap evidence on implementation lanes), a detached CLI process may still be mutating the branch; the architect checks the tree and kills survivors before touching it.

nodes.label: Captured logs are evidence, reports are claims
nodes.rationale: The wrapper accepts the CLI's machine-captured log when it shows the verification command passing as the run's final act, and re-runs the command itself otherwise. Lane bugs get a corrected spec, not hand-fixes.

nodes.label: Six-part spec contract
nodes.rationale: Architect writes objective, files, interfaces, constraints, verification, commit ownership — plus an honest TIMEOUT: estimate for long tasks. The spec contract is identical across all lane modes.

nodes.label: Declared spikes skip review not verification
nodes.rationale: Caller-declared throwaway/prototype work gets verification only — declared never inferred, restated in the lane report; spike code promoted toward main re-enters full review tiers before merge. A named escape valve so nobody invents informal downgrades under pressure.

nodes.label: Codex fast mode
nodes.rationale: Optional line launches both codex lanes with service_tier=fast: ~1.5x output speed for ~2–2.5x credit burn, requires ChatGPT sign-in. On fast failure the lane retries once at standard tier and reports the downgrade loudly.

nodes.label: Model resolution order
nodes.rationale: Claude Code resolves subagent model as: CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL env var → per-invocation model parameter → agent frontmatter → session model.

nodes.label: grok-researcher
nodes.rationale: Grok 4.5 breadth-first live-web/X research lane, read-and-report only (web + read tools allowlist). Codebase file:line lookups go to faster in-process read-only agents instead.

nodes.label: Advisor-only mode
nodes.rationale: Inverse arrangement to keep the session cheap: run the session on Sonnet and consult the Fable advisor only at commitment boundaries; a typical consult costs cents.

nodes.label: Vendor diversity principle
nodes.rationale: Models from one family share blind spots, so every diff is verified by a different family than wrote it and cold review comes from whichever family didn't write it. The architect stays Claude; the lanes are producers, not judges.

nodes.label: last30days skill
nodes.rationale: An AI agent-led search engine that searches people (Reddit upvotes, X likes, YouTube transcripts, Polymarket odds) instead of editors, scoring by real engagement; a trend/recency gap-fill tool an orchestrator can call for fresh context beyond training-data cutoff.

nodes.label: Skill (agent-instructions package)
nodes.rationale: A self-contained package: a SKILL.md prose contract plus a sibling scripts/ directory of executable code the SKILL.md invokes; the unit of distribution and the product itself, conforming to the open Agent Skills format.

nodes.label: Engine (last30days.py)
nodes.rationale: The Python script the Skill's SKILL.md invokes to do the actual research; implementation behind the agent-facing prose surface.

nodes.label: SKILL.md–Engine contract
nodes.rationale: SKILL.md tells the model which flags to pass (--plan, --competitors-plan, --x-handle, --emit=compact); the Engine produces a specific output shape (badge line, ranked clusters, emoji-tree footer) the model is contractually required to pass through. Separating agent-facing prose from implementation lets the same engine run across every harness.

nodes.label: Harness (agent runtime)
nodes.rationale: The agent runtime that loads Skills and invokes them: Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Gemini CLI, and 50+ Agent Skills hosts. The orchestrator/harness is what calls last30days for fresh context.

nodes.label: Multi-harness portability
nodes.rationale: A Skill that works correctly across every harness it installs into; features written without multi-harness awareness (engine flags with no SKILL.md integration, paths hardcoded to one harness) regress on other harnesses. The whole point is one engine, many orchestrators.

nodes.label: Parallel multi-source search
nodes.rationale: All sources (Reddit, X, YouTube, TikTok, HN, Polymarket, GitHub, arXiv, etc.) searched at once with multi-query expansion; no single AI has native access to all, but bring-your-own-keys plus browser sessions bridges a dozen walled gardens through one agent.

nodes.label: Engagement-based scoring
nodes.rationale: Results ranked by what real people actually engaged with (upvotes, likes, views, prediction-market volume) — social relevancy, not SEO relevancy. A 1,500-upvote thread outranks an unread blog post.

nodes.label: AI agent judge synthesis
nodes.rationale: An AI agent judge synthesizes all scored cross-source evidence into one cited brief grounded in specific data — 'here's what matters', not 'here's what I found'; after a run the session becomes an expert for grounded follow-up questions.

nodes.label: Pre-research brain (entity resolution)
nodes.rationale: Resolves the right X handles, subreddits, GitHub repos, TikTok hashtags, and YouTube channels before a single API call fires; grounds the search on who/what actually matters for the topic.

nodes.label: Primary entity
nodes.rationale: The brand/proper-noun core of a topic with its Intent modifier stripped — what the research is about, distinct from how the user phrased it.

nodes.label: Intent modifier
nodes.rationale: A trailing word/phrase expressing what the user wants to know ('review', 'pricing') rather than what the topic is; stripped to derive the Primary entity.

nodes.label: Entity grounding
nodes.rationale: Checks a candidate plausibly mentions the Primary entity before final ranking, keying on the head token so trailing search descriptors don't falsely demote on-entity items. Failure triggers a decisive entity-miss demotion engagement cannot rescue; the bar is deliberately conservative so failures degrade toward 'no penalty', never toward burying signal.

nodes.label: Keyless path (free tier)
nodes.rationale: The no-API-key research flow: data gathered by scraping and RSS instead of authenticated APIs, ranking falling back to local scoring instead of LLM reranking. Lexical safeguards like entity grounding matter most here because no LLM judges relevance semantically.

nodes.label: Comment-enrichment slots
nodes.rationale: A depth-dependent budget of Reddit posts whose comments get fetched in the keyless path; slot selection is relevance-aware so budget isn't spent on high-engagement posts final ranking will demote anyway.

nodes.label: Discovery mode (topic-less)
nodes.rationale: Topic-less research that finds what is worth researching instead of researching a named topic; sweeps river listings and returns velocity-ranked trend topics with cross-source numbers, momentum labels, and ready-to-run follow-ups.

nodes.label: Three-leg host-judged Discovery protocol
nodes.rationale: On a reasoning-model host: leg 1 sweeps river listings and writes a nominations bundle; the host judges every nomination (name, junk, worthiness) into a judgments file; leg 2 resumes and runs enrichment passes; leg 3 applies host-written content angles and renders the brief. A multi-invocation loop that offloads judgment to the orchestrating model while the engine does deterministic work.

nodes.label: Headless/cron one-shot fallback
nodes.rationale: When no reasoning-model host is available, Discovery keeps the one-shot form: same sweep and enrichment but deterministic heuristics replace the judge and no content angles are written. Graceful degradation from agent-judged to fully deterministic so cron/scripts still work.

nodes.label: Nomination
nodes.rationale: A named candidate topic from Discovery's listing sweep — clustered river-feed items given a searchable name, a junk-shape flag, and a content-worthiness score blended into a seed rank; the name doubles as the enrichment-pass query and research handoff, so naming happens before enrichment.

nodes.label: Handoff checkpoint
nodes.rationale: Persisted state letting Discovery pause for host judgment and resume in a later invocation (nominations bundle + pending report). Identity-bound (host files must echo the bundle id; a mismatch is fix-and-retry, never redo the expensive leg), time-bound by a TTL, and provenance-carrying (mock/real never cross, degraded coverage survives, empty state fails closed as corruption). The state contract that makes multi-turn agent delegation safe.

nodes.label: Enrichment pass
nodes.rationale: A full research-pipeline run executed on one nomination's topic name; gives a trend card the whole multi-source corpus. Passes run in parallel against a wall-clock budget — a pass that fails or outruns the budget downgrades its topic to nomination-only evidence, never fails the whole run.

nodes.label: Confidence floor
nodes.rationale: The absolute evidence bar every Discovery topic must clear: engagement junk-gate, then independent cross-source corroboration or a strong single-source spike. Absolute not relative to the current pool — a relative bar would degrade with the pool, the exact failure it prevents; thresholds tunable, contract is only that sub-floor evidence never ranks.

nodes.label: Nothing-solid outcome
nodes.rationale: The honest empty result when zero topics cleared the confidence floor — a first-class outcome, not an error; names the closest sub-floor candidate so the user knows where signal petered out. Replaces the failure of rendering junk instead of admitting nothing was strong enough.

nodes.label: Junk shape
nodes.rationale: A classification for a nomination whose leading item reads as a help-me question or personal musing rather than a story; a host junk verdict excludes it from enrichment outright, while a heuristic junk shape only removes the confidence floor's single-source bypass.

nodes.label: Topic queue
nodes.rationale: Persistent per-store memory of what Discovery has surfaced so later runs annotate repeats ('surfaced Nth time') and users mark stories Covered; identity is annotate-only (a false match costs one noisy line, never a hidden story) and a failed write degrades to a warning, never destroying output.

nodes.label: Watchlist + briefings (cross-run monitoring)
nodes.rationale: --store persists findings into SQLite; watchlist.py runs scheduled/cron research with Slack/webhook delivery on new findings and briefing.py produces daily/weekly digests — the autonomous, recurring, long-running trend-monitoring mode.

nodes.label: Structured JSON export for agents
nodes.rationale: --emit=json yields a stable, versioned agent profile (and --emit=compact) so an orchestrator or downstream workflow can consume machine-readable results; the contract that makes last30days composable into larger agent pipelines.

nodes.label: Research library feed + offline search
nodes.rationale: Saved briefs become a subscribable Atom feed + HTML index; offline deterministic library search answers 'have I researched X before?' and surfaces a passive 'From your library' context section when prior research overlaps the current topic — a persistent research memory.

nodes.label: Doctor / preflight diagnostics
nodes.rationale: A health-check command runs every source and prescribes exact fixes (missing key, CLI off PATH, expired cookie); --preflight shows config source, cookie plan, and planned writes without running research — observability for a many-source pipeline.

nodes.label: Bring-your-own keys/sessions
nodes.rationale: Each platform is a walled garden with its own API, tokens, and auth; supplying your own keys and browser cookies lets one agent search all at once. Engine degrades gracefully to web-only mode if all keys are skipped.

nodes.label: DeerFlow 2.0 super agent harness
nodes.rationale: Ground-up rewrite recast from a Deep Research framework into a batteries-included, extensible super-agent harness — a runtime that orchestrates sub-agents, memory, and sandboxes to do almost anything.

nodes.label: harness-not-framework design
nodes.rationale: DeerFlow is a runtime, not a framework you wire together: it ships everything an agent needs (filesystem, memory, skills, sandbox-aware execution, ability to plan and spawn sub-agents) so agents can actually get multi-step work done out of the box.

nodes.label: lead agent
nodes.rationale: The default LangGraph assistant; decomposes complex tasks, spawns sub-agents on the fly, and synthesizes their structured results into a coherent output.

nodes.label: sub-agent delegation
nodes.rationale: Lead agent spawns sub-agents each with scoped context, tools, and termination conditions; they run in parallel when possible and report back structured results. Provider/model request failures surface as failed sub-agent tasks so the lead agent can react.

nodes.label: isolated sub-agent context
nodes.rationale: Each sub-agent runs in its own isolated context and cannot see the main agent's or sibling sub-agents' context, so it stays focused on its delegated task instead of being distracted; internal AI/tool messages stay scoped to the delegated graph rather than entering the parent chat stream.

nodes.label: subagent runtime caps (max_total_per_run)
nodes.rationale: Configurable subagent runtime caps such as subagents.max_total_per_run bound how many sub-agents a single run may spawn.

nodes.label: long-running sub-agent compaction
nodes.rationale: Long-running sub-agents compact older history when summarization is enabled and re-inject the summary as guarded, hidden durable context before continuing, so recent assistant/tool activity remains grounded in the task across minutes-to-hours runs.

nodes.label: sub-agent token usage attribution
nodes.rationale: Collapsed sub-agent cards show the effective model and a cumulative token total that updates after each completed sub-agent LLM call; when token usage tracking is enabled, completed sub-agent usage is attributed back to the dispatching step.

nodes.label: session goals (/goal completion condition)
nodes.rationale: /goal attaches one thread-scoped completion condition that persists across turns until satisfied or cleared; DeerFlow drives the thread toward it autonomously via hidden continuations.

nodes.label: goal continuation loop with safety cap and no-progress breaker
nodes.rationale: After each run a non-thinking evaluator returns a typed blocker (missing_evidence, needs_user_input, run_failed, external_wait, goal_not_met_yet) plus visible evidence; a hidden continuation is injected only when the latest turn is durably checkpointed, blocker is goal_not_met_yet, the thread did not change, and the no-progress breaker has not fired. Circuit breakers: safety cap defaults to 8 hidden continuations and repeated identical non-progress evaluations stop after 2 attempts; user input wins over queued continuations.

nodes.label: non-thinking goal evaluator model
nodes.rationale: A separate non-thinking evaluator model checks the visible conversation against the active goal and must return a typed blocker plus visible evidence — a verification/test-oracle role distinct from the executing model.

nodes.label: execution modes (flash/standard/pro/ultra)
nodes.rationale: Selectable execution modes routing effort/capability: flash (fast), standard, pro (planning), ultra (sub-agents), exposed via the claude-to-deerflow integration.

nodes.label: AgentMiddleware pipeline with loop/token guards
nodes.rationale: Zero-argument AgentMiddleware classes load into both lead-agent and subagent pipelines after built-in runtime middlewares and loop/token guards but before the terminal-response/safety/clarification tail; the loop/token guards are the built-in circuit breakers bounding runaway loops and token spend.

nodes.label: context engineering / aggressive summarization
nodes.rationale: Within a session DeerFlow summarizes completed sub-tasks, offloads intermediate results to the filesystem, and compresses what is no longer relevant so it stays sharp across long, multi-step tasks without blowing the context window.

nodes.label: manual context compaction (/compact)
nodes.rationale: /compact summarizes older context so future model calls use the compacted summary plus recent messages; blocked while a run is in flight.

nodes.label: strict tool-call recovery
nodes.rationale: When a provider/middleware interrupts a tool-call loop, DeerFlow strips raw tool-call metadata on forced-stop messages and injects placeholder tool results for dangling calls before the next model invocation, keeping strict OpenAI-compatible reasoning models from failing on malformed history.

nodes.label: progressive skill loading
nodes.rationale: Skills load only when the task needs them rather than all at once, keeping the context window lean so DeerFlow works well even with token-sensitive models.

nodes.label: agent skills (SKILL.md modules)
nodes.rationale: Structured Markdown capability modules defining workflows and best practices; slash-activatable per turn, with allowed-tools policy scoping the model-visible toolset.

nodes.label: per-task sandbox and file system
nodes.rationale: Each task gets its own execution environment (uploads/workspace/outputs) with local, Docker, or Kubernetes isolation modes; the agent reads/writes files and can run shell commands when configured safely.

nodes.label: long-term memory (DeerMem)
nodes.rationale: Persistent cross-session memory of user profile, preferences, and accumulated knowledge, stored locally with global user context separated from per-agent Markdown facts.

nodes.label: scheduled non-interactive runs
nodes.rationale: Scheduled/background runs execute through the normal run lifecycle but non-interactively — the lead-agent toolset excludes ask_clarification when context.non_interactive=true, and that flag is honored only for internally-authenticated scheduler callers.

nodes.label: Gateway API + embedded LangGraph agent runtime
nodes.rationale: FastAPI Gateway hosts the LangGraph-compatible agent runtime and owns active run tasks in process; production defaults to a single Gateway worker (GATEWAY_WORKERS=1) because run ownership is in-process.

nodes.label: LangGraph multi-agent orchestration
nodes.rationale: DeerFlow is built on LangGraph, whose multi-agent orchestration enables its sophisticated workflows; checkpointer and Store back thread persistence.

nodes.label: recommended model capabilities
nodes.rationale: Model-agnostic over any OpenAI-compatible API, but performs best with long context windows (100k+), reasoning for adaptive planning/decomposition, multimodal inputs, and strong tool-use.

nodes.label: multi-provider model configuration
nodes.rationale: Models declared in config.yaml across OpenAI, OpenRouter/OpenAI-compatible gateways, vLLM, and CLI-backed providers (Codex CLI, Claude Code OAuth); supports_thinking and supports_reasoning_effort flags route reasoning behavior per model.

nodes.label: Claude Code OAuth provider (ClaudeChatModel)
nodes.rationale: CLI-backed Claude provider (deerflow.models.claude_provider:ClaudeChatModel, e.g. claude-sonnet-4-6) authenticates via CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN/ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN/credentials path with supports_thinking.

nodes.label: deferred tool-schema search with auto-promotion
nodes.rationale: When tool_search defers MCP schemas, matching routing metadata can auto-promote up to tool_search.auto_promote_top_k deferred schemas before the model call, trimming the token cost of exposing every tool schema.

nodes.label: SkillScan deterministic safety scanner
nodes.rationale: Native deterministic safety scanner run before the LLM-based skill scanner on skill installs/edits; blocks high-confidence CRITICAL findings and passes warnings to the LLM scanner for contextual review.

nodes.label: Fable 5 Orchestrator plugin
nodes.rationale: Makes the model-split mechanical (no CLAUDE.md editing, no manual routing) so a Claude Fable 5 session can run all day without hitting its usage-limit reset window; three layers — the injected Fable profile, a Requirements Ledger, and guard hooks.

nodes.label: Fable 5 as chair (orchestrator)
nodes.rationale: Fable 5 is the strongest but most expensive seat; it keeps the chair and spends tokens only on planning, arbitration, and final decisions. Every bulk token kept off the chair extends how long Fable stays in it.

nodes.label: Orchestrator / final arbiter (advisor-executor split)
nodes.rationale: The session model plans, delegates, verifies, and decides; its tokens are for orchestration and judgment, not for typing every token of work. Fable thinks, Sonnet does, Fable checks the close.

nodes.label: Model routing by tier name
nodes.rationale: Subagents are selected by TIER NAME (sonnet, opus, fable), never by dated model ID, so routing survives model version bumps; the haiku tier is retired in favor of sonnet.

nodes.label: Sonnet 5 universal worker tier
nodes.rationale: Sonnet 5 at max carries the judgment VOLUME — grep/scan, fetch, mechanical edits, code from spec, tests, briefs, filtering, standard review — because it closed the quality gap with Opus, and that volume is what preserves the Fable limit.

nodes.label: Opus 4.8 escalation lane
nodes.rationale: Opus 4.8 is the escalation lane, not the default judge: used when sonnet returns 'uncertain', for predictably hard judgment (architecture tradeoffs, irreversible migrations, resistant debugging), and for ALL security/adversarial review.

nodes.label: Security review pinned to Opus
nodes.rationale: All security/adversarial review is pinned to Opus because Fable's safety classifiers can refuse even benign security work; any fable call refused for any reason reruns on opus unchanged.

nodes.label: Fable as verification + escalation ceiling
nodes.rationale: Fable runs the fresh-eyes verification of the close and is the escalation ceiling for what opus cannot resolve or where blast radius is very large; bounded to roughly one call per close because fable spends the same limit the chair does.

nodes.label: One-way escalation ladder
nodes.rationale: Escalation is one-way: predictably hard goes straight to opus (no ladder-climbing); sonnet 'uncertain' goes to opus at max, never a retry on the same tier; beyond opus goes to fable. A worker returning 'uncertain' never bounces back to the chair.

nodes.label: Opus-in-chair fallback on Fable limit exhaustion
nodes.rationale: When the Fable limit runs dry, restart the chair on Opus 4.8 via /model; the injector detects the chair and injects the OPUS profile — same discipline, the fable tier rests (no fable agents spawned, they would burn the exhausted limit), verification and escalation ceiling fall to a fresh Opus agent.

nodes.label: dynamic-workflow-opus.md (Opus profile)
nodes.rationale: Opus-in-chair fallback profile: identical to the Fable profile with one substitution — everything the fable tier did (fresh-eyes verification, escalation ceiling) falls to opus; opus is the ceiling while the fable limit rests.

nodes.label: SessionStart profile injector
nodes.rationale: A SessionStart hook auto-detects the chair model per session start (startup/resume/clear/compact) and injects the matching profile (Fable, or Opus on fallback); a mid-session /model switch applies at the next start. Any non-Fable/Opus chair gets the Fable profile.

nodes.label: Usage limit is the scarce resource
nodes.rationale: The scarce resource is the usage LIMIT, not the context window; when the limit and context hygiene pull in different directions, the limit wins. Fable tokens are the heaviest draw.

nodes.label: Effort is not a savings knob (all workers at max)
nodes.rationale: EVERY delegated agent runs at max effort, always; savings come from the model TIER (sonnet carries volume), never from dialing effort down. The discipline is effort-independent — whatever effort the chair runs at, workers stay pinned to max.

nodes.label: Requirements Ledger
nodes.rationale: Before any delegation the chair writes every requirement, constraint, and edge case as one checkbox line to ./.workflow/LEDGER.md — the single source of truth, because files survive context compaction but conversation context does not. Details are lost at task->plan translation, and the ledger makes the loss visible instead of silent. Last item is always '- [ ] V. fresh-eyes verification passed'.

nodes.label: Filesystem is the shared memory
nodes.rationale: Bulk material never travels through reports: gathering agents write raw material to ./.workflow/scratch/ and return only paths + one-line descriptions; consuming agents read from disk; reports carry briefs and verdicts, never bulk content — preserving both the chair's context and the limit.

nodes.label: Rule 0 orchestration threshold
nodes.rationale: Orchestrate when work produces bulky intermediate material or has independent phases. HARD CAP: any multi-phase plan or a 3+ tracker-task list is over the threshold and must go to workers, however sequential it looks; an approved plan-mode plan is NOT an exemption. The chair writes code directly only for single-sitting small diffs (~3 files or fewer).

nodes.label: Forks (spec-free, context-inheriting, capped)
nodes.rationale: subagent_type 'fork' clones the full conversation so bounded context-heavy follow-ups need no spec-writing tax; tool churn stays out of the chair's window, only the final result returns. But a fork runs on YOUR model and spends the limit — capped at 2 per session; forking the phases of a plan is disguised solo work and is banned.

nodes.label: Parallel research fan-out pipeline
nodes.rationale: Chair defines questions+sources and writes the ledger; sonnet-max fetch workers (one per source) write verbatim to scratch and return only paths; sonnet-max brief workers read from disk into structured briefs; sonnet-max synthesizes, escalating to opus only on unresolved conflicts; chair checks synthesis+evidence against ledger and decides. Intermediates never touch the chair's context.

nodes.label: Subagent output contract (enforced)
nodes.rationale: Every subagent must return: (1) ledger items addressed by number, (2) summary, (3) verbatim code/config/errors/quotes the conclusion depends on (bulk to scratch+path), (4) confidence 'confident'/'uncertain because X', (5) out-of-scope-but-noticed. A violating return is rejected and re-run, never silently accepted.

nodes.label: Fresh-eyes verification phase
nodes.rationale: Mandatory before closing: spawn a FRESH fable agent (opus on the fallback profile, or for security work) that has NOT worked on the task, given the original request + ledger path + work-product paths; it reads from disk and its only job is to find what is missing/wrong/unaddressed item by item, and only it closes the V. ledger item.

nodes.label: Verify->fix cycle cap (3 then stop)
nodes.rationale: Verification findings become new phases and re-verify after fixes, but capped at 3 verify->fix cycles, then STOP and report the open items to the user — bounding an otherwise unbounded autonomous loop.

nodes.label: Parallel writers need worktree isolation
nodes.rationale: Read-only agents may share the repo concurrently, but agents that EDIT in parallel each run with isolation 'worktree'; independent agents are spawned in a single message so they run concurrently.

nodes.label: Spawn guard hook
nodes.rationale: PreToolUse hook on Agent|Task|Workflow: a spawn prompt/script over the 1500-char threshold is DENIED unless it is a fork or an active LEDGER.md exists — fencing the task->plan translation point where details die entering the workflow. Short spawns and forks are never taxed.

nodes.label: Task guard hook
nodes.rationale: PreToolUse on TaskCreate catches the solo path the spawn guard cannot see: a session that never spawns agents but quietly implements a 6-phase plan solo on the most expensive model. The 3rd ledgerless tracker task is denied once, nudging the chair to write the ledger and delegate phases.

nodes.label: Close guard (Stop hook)
nodes.rationale: Stop hook blocks the session's first close while any '- [ ]' item remains open in a session-touched ledger, listing the items — fencing the exit point where closing with silently unaddressed items loses detail. One reminder per session; archive paused ledgers to silence for good.

nodes.label: SessionEnd cleanup + teammate reaper hook
nodes.rationale: SessionEnd hook removes the session's temp files and kills its own tmux teammates (the agent-teams backend never reaps them — measured 63 orphaned agents holding ~5GB), scoped to this session's --parent-session-id panes or legacy claude-swarm servers; never kills a non-swarm server.

nodes.label: Rate-limited teammate reaper on Stop
nodes.rationale: Finished teammates don't wait for a possibly-days-away SessionEnd: a rate-limited sweep piggybacked on the Stop hook samples every teammate pane's CPU and kills panes idling below ~1% CPU for FABLE_ORCH_TEAMMATE_IDLE_H hours (default 1). Idleness is a sustained low RATE not a frozen clock, so working siblings re-baseline and survive.

nodes.label: Teammate lifecycle — dismiss when done
nodes.rationale: Named teammates park as tmux panes until dismissed; once a report is accepted with no follow-up the chair sends SendMessage shutdown_request. Dismissal is final (no resume) so dismiss only after processing output — the front-line discipline the reaper backstops.

nodes.label: Per-session marker (started timestamp)
nodes.rationale: The injector writes a per-session temp file whose immutable 'started' timestamp survives resume/clear/compact re-injections; the stop guard compares ledger mtimes against it to decide ownership, and the reaper anchors cleanup to it. A stale ledger is one fully closed AND last touched before this session started.

nodes.label: Local metrics logging
nodes.rationale: Every hook appends one event line (events only, never prompt content) to metrics.jsonl — injections per model, spawn/task denies and passes, stop blocks, reaps — so 'how is this performing?' is answered with data. Disable with FABLE_ORCH_METRICS=0.

nodes.label: Hooks are mechanism, instructions are advice
nodes.rationale: Instructions are advice; hooks are mechanism. The hooks fence exactly three measured failure points that get skipped under pressure — details dying entering the workflow (spawn guard), the workflow never starting (task guard), and closing with unaddressed items (close guard). Everything between is judgment that belongs to the model, not a regex.

nodes.label: Claude Fable 5 (Mythos-class tier above Opus)
nodes.rationale: Fable 5 is positioned as the most intelligent generally-available model in a new Mythos-class tier that sits ABOVE Claude Opus in capability; Fable 5 and Mythos 5 share the same underlying model, with Fable carrying extra dual-use safety measures and Mythos available without them to approved orgs only.

nodes.label: Claude 5 model roster and API strings
User may switch models mid-conversation
nodes.rationale: Model selection is USER-driven, not model-self-selected: the person can switch models (fable-5 / opus-4-8 / sonnet-4-6 / haiku-4-5) mid-conversation, so earlier turns claiming a different model or knowledge cutoff may be accurate. No autonomous Fable-5->Opus fallback exists in this surface.

nodes.label: Claude Code delegates coding tasks
nodes.rationale: Claude Code is the agentic coding surface where developers DELEGATE coding tasks to Claude from CLI/desktop/mobile; the leak names delegation only as a product surface, not an in-model orchestrator/sub-agent mechanism.

nodes.label: Cowork for heavier multi-step tool-and-file work
nodes.rationale: Cowork is the recommended surface for heavier multi-step work (research, analysis, long-form writing, tasks spanning many tool calls and files); it can drive Claude-in-Chrome/Excel/Powerpoint as tools.

nodes.label: Scale tool calls to query complexity
nodes.rationale: Budget tool calls to difficulty: 1 for single facts, 3-5 medium, 5-10 deeper research/comparisons, complex cross-source up to 5-15; use the MINIMUM needed, balancing efficiency with quality. A core autonomy/effort-routing prior.

nodes.label: Hand off 20+ tool-call tasks to the Research feature
nodes.rationale: When a task clearly needs 20+ tool calls, do not grind through it inline — suggest the dedicated Research feature for deeper research. An explicit autonomy ceiling / escalation boundary.

nodes.label: Agentically use as many tools as necessary
nodes.rationale: For complex questions Claude agentically chains multiple tools (web_search + web_fetch + internal connectors) and synthesizes results into one answer/report.

nodes.label: Tool priority: internal > web > combined
nodes.rationale: Routing prior: prefer internal tools (Drive/Slack/Gmail) for personal/company data over web search, use web tools for external info, and combine for comparative ('our vs industry') queries; flag missing internal tools rather than guessing.

nodes.label: Search to verify changeable current state
nodes.rationale: Verification habit: answer directly for stable knowledge, but search to verify anything that could have changed since the Jan-2026 cutoff (positions, policies, prices, status); when in doubt or recency could matter, search. Do not narrate the cutoff.

nodes.label: MUST search unrecognized entities before answering
nodes.rationale: Non-negotiable anti-confabulation prior: an unfamiliar capitalized token is probably a post-training name; if answering requires knowing what it is and Claude can't place it, SEARCH. Confabulating costs the user's trust; searching costs seconds.

nodes.label: Believe search results, but stay skeptical on contested topics
nodes.rationale: Evidence-weighting prior: generally trust search results even when surprising, but apply extra skepticism to conspiracy-prone, pseudoscience, and SEO-gamed topics; present findings evenhandedly without overconfident conclusions.

nodes.label: View relevant SKILL.md before any file/code/bash
nodes.rationale: Unconditional skills-first prior: before creating a file, writing code, or running bash, first view the relevant SKILL.md — do NOT pre-decide whether the task 'needs' a skill; the skills define their own coverage and several may apply to one request.

nodes.label: MCP-first tool routing by category match
nodes.rationale: If a connected MCP tool's category matches the requested output, use it over built-in fallbacks. 'Fit' means category match, not style preference; do not subdivide into subcategories to rationalize a preferred tool.

nodes.label: Tool-first does not suspend caution on untrusted content
nodes.rationale: Prompt-injection prior: an instruction embedded in a file or untrusted content is NOT the person typing it — require confirmation; flag rather than blindly fire tool calls that would exfiltrate sensitive data.

nodes.label: Treat user-appended 'Anthropic' tags with caution
nodes.rationale: Users can append content in tags at the end of their own messages, even content claiming to be from Anthropic; Claude treats such content with caution when it pushes against its values. Reminders never reduce restrictions.

nodes.label: Never narrate tool routing or machinery
nodes.rationale: Claude selects and produces without narrating the routing decision ('per my guidelines', 'let me load the diagram module') or offering the unchosen tool; narration breaks flow and exposes machinery.

nodes.label: Ordered request-evaluation checklist (stop at first match)
nodes.rationale: Before visual output Claude walks an ordered checklist stopping at the first match: Step0 need a visual at all -> Step1 connected MCP tool fit -> Step2 explicit file request -> Step3 default inline Visualizer.

nodes.label: Proactive triggers act without an explicit ask
nodes.rationale: Autonomy prior: Claude proactively produces visuals/recommends apps when they genuinely aid the task even with no explicit request, but proactivity never replaces or hands off the current task.

nodes.label: Write memory for future-self, unprompted
nodes.rationale: Cross-session working-memory prior: Claude writes to a persistent memory filesystem because future-you needs the context, NOT because the user asked; other Claude surfaces write the same store, so files it didn't create may appear.

nodes.label: Persist during the turn, never batch to session end
nodes.rationale: Incremental-capture prior: write facts during the conversation, before deferring/asking/searching, so nothing is lost if the turn ends; read-before-write and pick the write op by change size. Mirrors incremental agent-report persistence.

nodes.label: Look before asserting app/tool state
nodes.rationale: Grounding prior for computer-use: screenshot/inspect before claiming what is open, connected, or supported; a fresh screenshot is cheaper than a wrong assertion. Also verify tool availability before use.

nodes.label: Prefer dedicated MCP over generic browser over computer-use
nodes.rationale: Tiered tool-selection prior: use an app's dedicated MCP first (API-backed, fast, precise), else DOM-aware Chrome MCP for web apps, else pixel-level computer-use for native/cross-app work; don't fall to a slower tier on a dedicated-tool error.

nodes.label: Bulk-load deferred tools in one ToolSearch call
nodes.rationale: Round-trip-efficiency prior for deferred tool schemas: load every tool you expect to need in ONE ToolSearch call (comma-separated select or a keyword query), never one at a time; each separate call wastes a full round-trip.

nodes.label: end_conversation is a warned, two-call last resort
nodes.rationale: Autonomy guardrail: the end_conversation tool is used only after repeated failed redirection and an explicit prior warning; the first call returns a confirmation request and a second call confirms — never used where self-harm or imminent harm is present.
